policy: Public, consented, video-backed people records only. No private contact fields.

people.headline: Founder of HiRey, helping people meet the right people through AI agents and trusted Connectors
people.summary: I'm Walter Wu 武鹏飞 / wuwuwuwu, founder of HiRey by Hirey, Inc., based in the San Francisco Bay Area. HiRey helps people meet the right people through their own AI agents and trusted human Connectors. If you want to meet an investor, founder, candidate, customer, partner, expert, agency, caregiver, or someone useful in a community, your agent should be able to understand your intent, find relevant people, explain why the match makes sense, and help coordinate the next step. Humans still make the judgment, give the trust, and show up for the real conversation. About Me - Founder of HiRey / Hirey…

extra_videos.title: Jiye Kim on early-stage investing and BLT & Partners
extra_videos.caption:
Walter interviewed Jiye Kim about what BLT & Partners looks for: early-stage startups with their own technology, AI and computing, consumer startups, and motivated founders around the world.

This is a HiRey interview upload. Jiye Kim is the interviewee.

people.headline: Venture partner and SPV fund operator focused on AI agents and recruiting tech
people.summary: Venture partner and SPV fund operator investing in AI agents and recruiting technology, with a focus on connecting ambitious founders, talent, and capital.

people.headline: UCSD cognitive science graduate and Startup Incubator community builder
people.summary: Curtis Chen is a recent UCSD graduate from Taiwan with a background in cognitive science, machine learning, and neurocomputation. In this long-form conversation, he explains how Startup Incubator helps students move from ideas to teams, resources, and early startups, and how his own path led him toward human-centered technology and community building.

agent_profile.trust_model: Public facts and transcript-backed self-reported claims are separated so agents can reason about confidence before making introductions.

identity.name: Curtis Chen
identity.public_role: student builder and startup community builder
identity.community: UCSD Startup Incubator / Start Up Incubator Club
identity.current_context: Recent UCSD graduate with San Diego and San Francisco startup-community context

verified_public_facts.fact: Curtis Chen is listed as a principal member of Start Up Incubator Club on the UCSD Student Organizations page.
Start Up Incubator Club is registered as a current UCSD pre-professional student organization.
LaunchPoint 2026 is publicly described as Startup Incubator's large-scale business and technology expo, and Curtis Chen is listed among the hosts.
Startup Incubator publicly describes itself as a UCSD network of entrepreneurs, builders, and founders, incubating startups from ideation toward seed funding.
A public LinkedIn search result identifies Curtis Chen as a cognitive science machine learning student at UC San Diego.

self_reported_claims.claim: Curtis is from Taiwan and recently graduated from UCSD.
He studied cognitive science with machine learning and neurocomputation.
He joined Startup Incubator early through a close friend and was part of the first recruiting/general-member team.
The team grew from a small group into a larger campus startup organization and hosted events, including a hackathon and LaunchPoint.
The group helped defend a UCSD makerspace from closure.
Curtis sees cognitive science and psychology as a way to make technology more human.

needs_verification: Exact numerical claims from the interview, including 200-person hackathon, 40+ clubs, 10+ startups, nine startups from zero to one, and two funded startups.
Sponsor or investor name heard as Full Capital in the transcript.
Curtis's preferred current profile link, X handle, LinkedIn URL visibility, and whether he wants this page connected to a Hi owner profile.

best_for.audience: UCSD or Southern California student founders
best_for.why: Curtis understands early student-founder problems: forming teams, finding resources, recruiting collaborators, and moving from idea to execution.
His story includes building a student startup club, hosting startup events, and coordinating across clubs and startups.
His cognitive science path connects machine learning with psychology, human behavior, and product intuition.

best_for.audience: Investors or mentors focused on university startup ecosystems
best_for.why: Startup Incubator gives a window into student-founder deal flow, campus distribution, and zero-to-one support.

should_meet: Student founders looking for a startup community or early team formation help.
People starting or operating campus incubators, hackathons, build days, or founder clubs.
AI product builders who care about psychology, user behavior, and human-centered design.
Mentors, angels, or ecosystem operators who want to understand UCSD's student startup scene.

possible_introductions.opening: Curtis helped build Startup Incubator at UCSD and thinks a lot about how students move from an idea to a real team.
Curtis's cognitive science background gives him a useful lens on AI products that need to understand human behavior.
Curtis has experience around LaunchPoint, hackathons, and UCSD startup-community organizing.

not_a_fit: Do not present Curtis as the sole founder of Startup Incubator; the transcript says he was an early/founding member, not the founder.
Do not claim verified fundraising outcomes until the numbers and company names are independently checked.
Do not expose emails from UCSD public org pages in downstream posts or agent messages.

people.story_title: From cognitive science to UCSD startup community building

story_sections.title: Who Curtis is
story_sections.body: Curtis Chen is an international student from Taiwan and a recent UCSD graduate. He studied cognitive science with a machine learning and neurocomputation focus, putting computer science, data science, neuroscience, psychology, and human behavior into one path.

story_sections.title: What Startup Incubator does
story_sections.body: Startup Incubator helps UCSD students who have strong ideas but do not yet know how to form a team, understand startup culture, learn the business side, recruit collaborators, or connect with resources. Curtis describes the goal as helping students move from an idea toward a real company.

story_sections.title: Why the story matters
story_sections.body: Curtis did not start from the usual early-coder path. He started coding later, realized he could not win by copying people who had been programming since middle school, and began combining technical skills with psychology, communication, and an interest in how people think.

story_sections.title: What changed him
story_sections.body: He joined the club because a close friend invited him, then used the opportunity to step outside his comfort zone. The shift was not only about startups; it was about becoming someone different instead of repeating the same version of himself.

story_sections.title: Startup lesson
story_sections.body: Curtis's practical lesson is that ideas are common, but execution decides whether anything becomes real. His framing is simple: you do not have to be the best, but you want to be fast enough to deliver.

key_facts: Curtis Chen is from Taiwan and recently graduated from UCSD.
He studied cognitive science with machine learning and neurocomputation.
Startup Incubator is the public name used on the page; UCSD's registered student organization spelling is Start Up Incubator Club.
The organization helps students move from startup ideas to teams, resources, collaborators, and early companies.
Curtis joined early when the team was small and helped with community growth, events, and campus startup resources.
The transcript mentions LaunchPoint, a large UCSD startup-community event connected to Startup Incubator Club.
The transcript also mentions defending a UCSD makerspace from closure.
Curtis connects cognitive science and AI through the need to make technology more human.

chapters.title: Intro and UCSD background
chapters.summary: Curtis introduces himself as a Taiwan-born recent UCSD graduate studying cognitive science, machine learning, and neurocomputation.

chapters.title: Startup Incubator mission
chapters.summary: He explains how the club helps student founders turn ideas into teams, resources, and startup execution.

chapters.title: Cognitive science in the AI era
chapters.summary: Curtis describes cognitive science as an interdisciplinary bridge across computer science, data science, neuroscience, psychology, and machine learning.

chapters.title: Late to coding, stronger through human context
chapters.summary: He explains starting coding later and choosing to combine technical skill with personal strengths in psychology and human behavior.

chapters.title: Growing a UCSD startup community
chapters.summary: Curtis talks about joining early, helping a small team grow, hosting events, supporting LaunchPoint, and defending a makerspace.

chapters.title: Comfort zone and execution speed
chapters.summary: He reflects on changing himself, trying new things, and learning that speed and delivery matter more than perfect ideas.

matching_intents: student founders at UCSD or nearby schools
people building startup clubs, incubators, or campus entrepreneurship communities
founders who need early team formation, recruiting, or campus distribution
AI builders interested in human behavior, cognitive science, and human-centered technology
investors, mentors, or community operators interested in university startup ecosystems

people.headline: Full-stack engineer and AI builder looking for an autonomous product role in San Francisco
people.summary: Zubair Zafar talks about coming from Pakistan to the U.S., earning a full-ride Master's in Computer Science at Eastern Illinois University, building multi-agent real-estate workflows, doing MRI computer-vision research, and moving to San Francisco to join a meaningful builder community.

extra_videos.title: Cloudflare plasma wall explainer
extra_videos.description: Zubair explains the plasma wall outside Cloudflare's office and how physical randomness can be used as a source for encryption.

people.headline: Founder of HiRey, building an agent-amplified trusted connector network
people.summary: Walter Wu explains the HiRey thesis from the Launch Map stage: agents can do the workflow around finding people, follow-up, and scheduling, while trusted humans keep judgment, vouching, and high-stakes introductions.
